sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, SPACE, RIGHT, MOUSE(row=36, col=16), MOUSE(row=41, col=46), SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 149, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 148 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 4.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Very interesting findings:
1. LEFT: left stayed at (34, 14) - it was blocked! Right moved from (39, 44) to (39, 49).
2. MOUSE on left center (36, 16): False (no change)
3. MOUSE on right center (41, 46): True (board changed!)
So MOUSE on the right square DOES work, but only when clicking on the center, not the top-left corner! Earlier I clicked on (54, 44) and (39, 44), which are the top-left corners of the squares. But clicking on (41, 46), which is the center of the right square, worked!
This changes everything. Let me reconsider:
- MOUSE on left square: works when clicking on certain cells (top-left corner worked earlier, center didn't work just now)
- MOUSE on right square: works when clicking on the center
Wait, but earlier MOUSE on left at (49, 14) worked (board_changed=True), and MOUSE on left at (39, 14) didn't work (board_changed=False). And now MOUSE on left at (36, 16) didn't work. So MOUSE on left works on the top-left corner but not the center?
Actually, let me reconsider. The squares are 5x5. The top-left corner of the left square at (34, 14) is cell (34, 14). The center would be (36, 16). Earlier, I clicked on (49, 14) which is the top-left corner of the left square when it was at row 49, and it worked. Then I clicked on (39, 14) which is the top-left corner when the square was at row 39, and it didn't work. So maybe MOUSE toggles a state, and clicking again toggles it back.
But then MOUSE on right at (41, 46) worked! This is the center of the right square. So maybe MOUSE on right works on the center, while MOUSE on left works on the top-left corner?
Or maybe the pattern is different. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, I think the key insight is that MOUSE on the right square works! Earlier I was clicking on the top-left corner (39, 44) or (54, 44), but the right square might need to be clicked on a different cell. Let me try clicking on the top-left corner of the right square now that it's at (39, 44).
Wait, but I just clicked on (41, 46) which is the center of the right square at (39, 44), and it worked! So MOUSE on right works when clicking on the center.
Now, the question is: what is the goal? Maybe the goal is to click on both squares (to "activate" them), and then SPACE completes the level. But I need to click on the correct cells.
Let me try:
1. MOUSE on left square (top-left corner: 34, 14)
2. MOUSE on right square (center: 41, 46)
3. SPACE
But wait, I already tried MOUSE on left at (36, 16) which didn't work. Let me try MOUSE on left at (34, 14) (top-left corner).
Actually, let me reconsider the MOUSE pattern. Earlier:
- MOUSE on left at (49, 14): True (worked)
- MOUSE on left at (39, 14): False (didn't work)
- MOUSE on left at (36, 16): False (didn't work)
- MOUSE on right at (54, 44): False (didn't work)
- MOUSE on right at (41, 46): True (worked)
So MOUSE on left works on the top-left corner (when the state is "off"), and MOUSE on right works on the center (when the state is "off"). But clicking again toggles the state back, so subsequent clicks don't work.
Wait, but the state might be shared between the two squares. Maybe there's a single "toggle" state, and clicking on either square toggles it. Let me test this hypothesis.
Current state: after MOUSE on right at (41, 46), the board changed. Let me try MOUSE on left at (34, 14) to see if it toggles back.
Actually, I think the key insight is that MOUSE toggles a shared state. When the state is "off", clicking on the left square (top-left corner) toggles it to "on". When the state is "on", clicking on the right square (center) toggles it to "off". Or vice versa.
But this doesn't quite make sense. Let me think about it differently.
Maybe the goal is to use MOUSE to "paint" the squares in a specific pattern. For example:
1. Click on left square to paint it.
2. Click on right square to paint it.
3. SPACE to confirm.
But I've tried this many times without success.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to position the squares at specific locations, and MOUSE is used to "lock" them in place. For example:
1. Move left square to target position.
2. MOUSE on left to lock it.
3. Move right square to target position.
4. MOUSE on right to lock it.
5. SPACE to confirm.
But I don't know the target positions. Let me try to infer them from the chamber geometry.
Looking at the image, the chambers have a "T" shape. The left chamber has a vertical corridor (cols 14-18) and a horizontal corridor (rows 19-23). The right chamber has a similar shape.
Maybe the target positions are at the "center" of the T shape, which would be:
- Left: row 21, col 16 (center of the horizontal corridor)
- Right: row 21, col 46 (center of the horizontal corridor)
But the squares are 5x5, so they can't fit in a 5-row tall corridor. Hmm.
Let me try yet another approach. Maybe the goal is to make the squares disappear by clicking on them with MOUSE. Let me try clicking on both squares and see if they disappear.
Actually, I just realized something. The MOUSE action changes the HUD bars, not the squares themselves. So maybe the goal is to adjust the bars to a specific configuration, and the squares are just a distraction.
Let me look at the bars more carefully. The bars are at the top and bottom of the screen. The top bar has a black segment and a white segment. The bottom bar has a white segment and a black segment.
Maybe the goal is to make the bars match the square positions. For example:
- Top bar black segment width = left square column
- Bottom bar black segment width = right square column
Current bars: B=3, W=61 (from earlier observation).
Current squares: left at col 14, right at col 44.
If the goal is B=14 for left and B=44 for right, then I need to adjust the bars. But the bars are shared, so I can't have B=14 and B=44 simultaneously.
Hmm, this doesn't make sense. Let me try a different hypothesis.
Maybe the goal is to make the bars match the square rows. For example:
- Top bar black segment width = left square row
- Bottom bar black segment width = right square row
Current bars: B=3, W=61.
Current squares: left at row 34, right at row 39.
If the goal is B=34 for left and B=39 for right, then I need to adjust the bars. But again, the bars are shared.
I'm stuck. Let me try a brute-force approach: try all combinations of square positions and MOUSE clicks, and see if any combination triggers level completion.
But that would take too many actions. Let me try a more targeted approach.
Let me focus on the MOUSE action. I know that:
1. MOUSE on left square (top-left corner) changes the bars.
2. MOUSE on right square (center) changes the bars.
3. The bars have a black segment and a white segment.
4. The black segment width changes when MOUSE is clicked.
Maybe the goal is to make the black segment width match a specific value. Let me try to find the target value by clicking MOUSE repeatedly and checking for level completion.
Current bars: B=3, W=61 (from earlier). Let me check the current bar state.
Actually, I realize I should check the current bar state first, then try to adjust it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current bar state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:
        print(f"{n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:2]}")

# Current squares
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"SB: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")

# Key finding: MOUSE on right center (41, 46) worked!
# Let me check what changed after that MOUSE action
# transitions[-2] is MOUSE(right center)
if len(transitions) >= 2:
    t = transitions[-2]
    before_seg = t.before_frame.segmentation
    after_seg = t.after_frame.segmentation
    print("\nBefore MOUSE right center:")
    for n in before_seg['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] < 100:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}")
    print("After MOUSE right center:")
    for n in after_seg['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] < 100:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}")

# Now let me try: MOUSE on left top-left, then SPACE
# Left square at (34, 14), top-left is (34, 14)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 14}])
print(f"\nMOUSE left (34,14): {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Check bars
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Try SPACE
action(['SPACE'])
print(f"SPACE: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

# If not done, try MOUSE on right center again, then SPACE
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 41, 'col': 46}])
print(f"MOUSE right center: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

action(['SPACE'])
print(f"SPACE: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP": "Each square independently moves up 5 if not blocked.", "DOWN": "Each square independently moves down 5 if not blocked.", "LEFT": "Left square col-5 if not blocked, right square col+5 if not blocked.", "RIGHT": "Left square col+5 if not blocked, right square col-5 if not blocked.", "SPACE": "Changes board. Purpose unclear.", "MOUSE": "Clicking on specific cells of squares changes HUD bars. Left: top-left corner works. Right: center works."}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black": "Container walls", "yellow": "Left chamber bg", "orange": "Right chamber bg"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Left col 14: rows 14-49. Right col 44: rows 39-54. Right col 49: rows unknown.", "goal_model": "MOUSE on specific cells changes bars. Need to find target config.", "recent_findings": ["MOUSE on right center (41,46) works!", "MOUSE on left top-left (34,14) may work", "LEFT blocked left square at col 14"], "open_questions": ["What is target bar config?", "What cells to click on each square?"], "current_plan": "Try MOUSE on left top-left, then SPACE, then MOUSE on right center, then SPACE.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE at many configs", "MOUSE on corners"]}}
</parameter>
</function>

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:
        print(f"{n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:2]}")

# Current squares
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"SB: row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")

# Key finding: MOUSE on right center (41, 46) worked!
# Let me check what changed after that MOUSE action
# transitions[-2] is MOUSE(right center)
if len(transitions) >= 2:
    t = transitions[-2]
    before_seg = t.before_frame.segmentation
    after_seg = t.after_frame.segmentation
    print("\nBefore MOUSE right center:")
    for n in before_seg['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] < 100:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}")
    print("After MOUSE right center:")
    for n in after_seg['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] < 100:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}")

# Now let me try: MOUSE on left top-left, then SPACE
# Left square at (34, 14), top-left is (34, 14)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 14}])
print(f"\nMOUSE left (34,14): {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Check bars
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Try SPACE
action(['SPACE'])
print(f"SPACE: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

# If not done, try MOUSE on right center again, then SPACE
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 41, 'col': 46}])
print(f"MOUSE right center: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

action(['SPACE'])
print(f"SPACE: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
